topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara slammed the laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the sterile office. She stared at the screen, the lines of code—the meticulously structured algorithms she’d spent the last three weeks perfecting—now mocking her. Across the shared workspace, she saw Liam, his head tilted in the exact same posture, his fingers hovering over a keyboard identical to hers. A slow, hot flush crept up her neck. "Are you serious?" she managed, her voice tight, laced with disbelief. Liam looked up, a flicker of something unreadable in his eyes, before he offered a small, almost patronizing smile. Elara pushed her chair back, the scrape of metal against the floor a deliberate, sharp sound. She gathered her notes, stuffing them roughly into her bag, and walked out, her pace brisk, refusing to look back at the mirrored effort.

[story 2]

I watched the progress bar complete, a wave of profound annoyance washing over me. It was finished. Perfectly finished. Then, I noticed the corner of the desk. Beside my monitor, another setup, an identical workstation, housing a project that mirrored mine down to the last line of configuration. A strange, cold certainty settled in my stomach. I leaned in, my jaw tightening. I didn't want to confront anyone; I just wanted to dismantle the illusion. I picked up my coffee mug, its ceramic warm against my palm, and deliberately set it down with a resounding clink. I straightened my posture, making sure my shoulders were squared, a silent declaration that this duplication was not going to derail my momentum.

[story 3]

The realization hit me like a physical blow. I was staring at the blueprint, the complex architecture I had painstakingly designed, and then I saw it—a nearly identical schematic displayed on the adjacent screen. My breath hitched. A sudden, intense heat flooded my chest, making my hands tremble slightly. I didn't speak. Instead, I rose slowly from my seat, walking toward the divider between the two workstations. I placed my hand flat on the partition, leaning in close enough to see the other person—a stranger wearing my own ambition. My gaze was unwavering, challenging, refusing to concede the ground.

The realization hit me like a physical blow. A sudden, intense heat flooded my chest, making my hands tremble slightly. I didn't speak. Instead, I rose slowly from my seat, walking toward the divider between the two workstations.
